claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex, who's 14, and Blake, who's 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask about Blake, who is 19 years old.

Is Blake currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in approved education. He's doing his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Now I need to understand your living situation and responsibility for the children.

Do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, in Manchester. Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't - Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I need to ask about your relationship with Alex, who lives with their other parent.

Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't currently contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me evaluate your situation for each child:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake, but not for Alex.**

**For Blake (19 years old):**
- ✅ **Eligible** - Even though Blake is 19, you still qualify because he is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Since Blake lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for him and can claim Child Benefit.

**For Alex (14 years old):**
- ❌ **Not Eligible** - Although Alex is under 16, you are not considered responsible for Alex under Child Benefit rules. This is because Alex lives with their other parent and you don't contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, and in this case, the other parent (who Alex lives with) would be the eligible claimant.

If you'd like to claim Child Benefit for Blake, you can proceed with an application. If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if you start contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep), your eligibility situation may be different.

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), so age-wise would qualify for Child Benefit. However, Alex does not live with you - Alex lives with their other parent. According to the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either you live with them OR you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep (on food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Since Alex doesn't live with you and you do not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, you are not considered responsible for Alex under the Child Benefit rules. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old, which is normally above the age limit of 16. However, you'll still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake is doing A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education. Blake also lives with you in the UK. Since Blake is under 20, in approved education, and lives with you, you are responsible for Blake and qualify for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only. Blake qualifies because he is under 20, in approved education (A-levels), and lives with you. You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because Alex lives with their other parent and you do not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, meaning you are not considered responsible for Alex under the rules. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, and the other parent would be the eligible claimant for Alex.

According to the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either you live with them OR you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep (on food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Since Alex doesn't live with you and you do not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, you are not considered responsible for Alex under the Child Benefit rules. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old, which is normally above the age limit of 16. However, you'll still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake is doing A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education. Blake also lives with you in the UK. Since Blake is under 20, in approved education, and lives with you, you are responsible for Blake and qualify for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only. Blake qualifies because he is under 20, in approved education (A-levels), and lives with you. You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because Alex lives with their other parent and you do not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, meaning you are not considered responsible for Alex under the rules. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, and the other parent would be the eligible claimant for Alex.
